message.content: let's look at the github.com/canonical/operator repository. you can use `gh` if needed. i want to review all the bug fixes that have landed in `main`. for some time, the repository has used conventional commits, so they will be the commits with a message that starts with `fix:` or `fix!:`. when you go back far enough that won't be the case and you'll need to figure out if the commit is a bug fix based in the commit message title and body. i want to produce a markdown document that summarises all the bug fixes operator has had. each summary should have a link to the commit, the commit title and summary, and a summary of the changes that you make looking at the actual diff. once we have all the individual summaries, we want to identify patterns, so keep thst in mind and possibly add more fields to the summaries to support that. when we have all the commits analysed, we want to produce a second markdown document that outlines the most common and most significant types of bugs that tend to be introduced in this repository, with some short examples and links to fixes, so that people or agents working in this repository know where rhe hard bits are and can develop more reliably. pay extra attention to anything that is security related, and prioritise the most significant issues (like crash on start). if it works better the first document can be a folder with one document for each commit. then we want a third deliverable that is made by using what we have put together previously and analysing the current state of the code (main@head) to see whether there seem to be cases of these bugs that still exist. this is a big job, you probabky want to write a plan. the current folder is empty, you cam use it for all your working. you are in a sandbox so can safely run code and so on.

content.text:
This is a big job — let me start by planning and getting the initial data. I'll work in phases:

1. **Clone & collect** — get the repo, extract all bug fix commits
2. **Analyze commits** — diff each one, write per-commit summaries
3. **Pattern analysis** — synthesize into a patterns document
4. **Current code audit** — check HEAD for latent instances of known bug patterns

Let me start by cloning and exploring the commit history.

input.command: gh repo clone canonical/operator -- --bare operator.git 2>&1 | tail -5

# Count commits before conventional commits era (before ~commit 1024)
# The oldest fix: commit is 942bd23 from 2023-10-04
# Let's see how many commits are before that
git log --oneline main --ancestry-path 929c9cc..942bd23 | wc -l

# Let's see when conventional commits actually started being used consistently
# Check around commit 1024 area
git log --oneline main --format="%ad %s" --date=short | sed -n '680,720p'

input.command:
# The transition is around mid-2024. Let me check how many non-conventional commits look like bug fixes
# First, let's get all commits that DON'T start with conventional prefixes, before the transition
git log --oneline main --format="%H %s" | grep -viE "^[a-f0-9]+ (fix|feat|chore|ci|docs|test|refactor|build|perf|style|revert)[(!:]" | head -30

content.text: Let me write a Python script to classify the non-conventional commits by keywords, then I'll use agents to do the heavy lifting of analyzing diffs.

input.content:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Classify non-conventional commits as likely bug fixes vs not."""
import re

BUG_KEYWORDS = [
    r'\bfix\b', r'\bfixed\b', r'\bfixes\b', r'\bfixing\b',
    r'\bbug\b', r'\bbugfix\b',
    r'\bcorrect\b', r'\bcorrected\b', r'\bcorrection\b',
    r'\bpatch\b', r'\bpatched\b',
    r'\bresolve[ds]?\b', r'\bworkaround\b',
    r'\bregression\b', r'\bbroken\b', r'\bbreak\b',
    r'\berror\b', r'\bcrash\b', r'\bfail\b',
    r'\bhandle\b.*\b(error|exception|edge|case)\b',
    r'\bprevent\b', r'\bavoid\b',
    r'\bensure\b', r'\bguard\b',
    r'\bwrong\b', r'\bincorrect\b', r'\binvalid\b',
    r'\bmissing\b', r'\btypo\b',
    r'\bnone\s*(check|guard|handling)\b',
    r'\bkey\s*error\b', r'\battribute\s*error\b', r'\btype\s*error\b',
    r'\boff.by.one\b', r'\brace\b', r'\bdeadlock\b',
    r'\bsecurity\b', r'\bvulnerab\b', r'\bexploit\b',
    r'\bsanitiz\b', r'\bescap\b',
    r'\bdon.t\b.*\b(crash|fail|error|break)\b',
    r'\bshould\b.*\bnot\b', r'\bshould\b.*\binstead\b',
    r'\bactually\b', r'\bproperly\b',
]

# Keywords that suggest NOT a bug fix
NOT_BUG_KEYWORDS = [
    r'^Merge\b', r'^Bump\b', r'^Update\b.*\bdep', r'^Add\s+(new\s+)?feature',
    r'^Remove\s+deprecated', r'^Reorganis', r'^Move\b',
    r'\brefactor\b', r'\bcleanup\b', r'\bclean up\b',
    r'\bdocument', r'\bREADME\b', r'\bchangelog\b',
    r'\bbump\s+version\b', r'\brelease\b',
]

likely_fixes = []
maybe_fixes = []
not_fixes = []

with open('/home/ubuntu/operstor-analysis/nonconventional_commits.txt') as f:
    for line in f:
        line = line.strip()
        if not line:
            continue
        parts = line.split('|||')
        if len(parts) < 3:
            continue
        sha, subject, date = parts[0], parts[1], parts[2]

        # Skip merge commits with no useful info

toolUseResult.content:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Classify non-conventional commits as likely bug fixes vs not."""
import re

BUG_KEYWORDS = [
    r'\bfix\b', r'\bfixed\b', r'\bfixes\b', r'\bfixing\b',
    r'\bbug\b', r'\bbugfix\b',
    r'\bcorrect\b', r'\bcorrected\b', r'\bcorrection\b',
    r'\bpatch\b', r'\bpatched\b',
    r'\bresolve[ds]?\b', r'\bworkaround\b',
    r'\bregression\b', r'\bbroken\b', r'\bbreak\b',
    r'\berror\b', r'\bcrash\b', r'\bfail\b',
    r'\bhandle\b.*\b(error|exception|edge|case)\b',
    r'\bprevent\b', r'\bavoid\b',
    r'\bensure\b', r'\bguard\b',
    r'\bwrong\b', r'\bincorrect\b', r'\binvalid\b',
    r'\bmissing\b', r'\btypo\b',
    r'\bnone\s*(check|guard|handling)\b',
    r'\bkey\s*error\b', r'\battribute\s*error\b', r'\btype\s*error\b',
    r'\boff.by.one\b', r'\brace\b', r'\bdeadlock\b',
    r'\bsecurity\b', r'\bvulnerab\b', r'\bexploit\b',
    r'\bsanitiz\b', r'\bescap\b',
    r'\bdon.t\b.*\b(crash|fail|error|break)\b',
    r'\bshould\b.*\bnot\b', r'\bshould\b.*\binstead\b',
    r'\bactually\b', r'\bproperly\b',
]

# Keywords that suggest NOT a bug fix
NOT_BUG_KEYWORDS = [
    r'^Merge\b', r'^Bump\b', r'^Update\b.*\bdep', r'^Add\s+(new\s+)?feature',
    r'^Remove\s+deprecated', r'^Reorganis', r'^Move\b',
    r'\brefactor\b', r'\bcleanup\b', r'\bclean up\b',
    r'\bdocument', r'\bREADME\b', r'\bchangelog\b',
    r'\bbump\s+version\b', r'\brelease\b',
]

likely_fixes = []
maybe_fixes = []
not_fixes = []

with open('/home/ubuntu/operstor-analysis/nonconventional_commits.txt') as f:
    for line in f:
        line = line.strip()
        if not line:
            continue
        parts = line.split('|||')
        if len(parts) < 3:
            continue
        sha, subject, date = parts[0], parts[1], parts[2]

        # Skip merge commits with no useful info
        if subject.startswith('Merge pull request') or subject.startswith('Merge branch') or subject.startswith('Merge remote'):
            not_fixes.append((sha, subject, date))
            continue

        subject_lower = subject.lower()

        # Check for NOT bug fix signals
        is_not_bug = False
        for pat in NOT_BUG_KEYWORDS:
            if re.search(pat, subject, re.IGNORECASE):
                is_not_bug = True
                break

        # Check for bug fix signals
        bug_score = 0
        for pat in BUG_KEYWORDS:
            if re.search(pat, subject_lower):
                bug_score += 1

        if bug_score >= 2:
            likely_fixes.append((sha, subject, date, bug_score))
        elif bug_score >= 1 and not is_not_bug:
            maybe_fixes.append((sha, subject, date, bug_score))
        else:
            not_fixes.append((sha, subject, date))

print(f"Likely fixes: {len(likely_fixes)}")
print(f"Maybe fixes: {len(maybe_fixes)}")
print(f"Not fixes: {len(not_fixes)}")

print("\n=== LIKELY FIXES ===")
for sha, subj, date, score in likely_fixes:
    print(f"{sha[:8]} [{date}] (score={score}) {subj}")

print("\n=== MAYBE FIXES ===")
for sha, subj, date, score in maybe_fixes:
    print(f"{sha[:8]} [{date}] (score={score}) {subj}")

# Write out the combined list for further processing
with open('/home/ubuntu/operstor-analysis/candidate_nonconv_fixes.txt', 'w') as f:
    for sha, subj, date, score in likely_fixes + maybe_fixes:
        f.write(f"{sha}|||{subj}|||{date}|||{score}\n")

input.content:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Extract commit data (message + diff) for all bug fix candidates."""
import subprocess
import json
import os

def get_commit_data(sha):
    """Get full commit message and diff stats for a commit."""
    # Full message
    msg = subprocess.run(
        ['git', 'log', '-1', '--format=%B', sha],
        capture_output=True, text=True
    ).stdout.strip()

    # Diff stat
    stat = subprocess.run(
        ['git', 'diff', '--stat', f'{sha}~1..{sha}', '--'],
        capture_output=True, text=True
    ).stdout.strip()

    # Full diff (limited to avoid huge diffs)
    diff = subprocess.run(
        ['git', 'diff', f'{sha}~1..{sha}', '--', '*.py'],
        capture_output=True, text=True
    ).stdout

    # Truncate very large diffs
    if len(diff) > 50000:
        diff = diff[:50000] + "\n... [TRUNCATED] ..."

    # Subject line
    subject = subprocess.run(
        ['git', 'log', '-1', '--format=%s', sha],
        capture_output=True, text=True
    ).stdout.strip()

    # Date
    date = subprocess.run(
        ['git', 'log', '-1', '--format=%ad', '--date=short', sha],
        capture_output=True, text=True
    ).stdout.strip()

    # Files changed
    files = subprocess.run(
        ['git', 'diff', '--name-only', f'{sha}~1..{sha}'],
        capture_output=True, text=True
    ).stdout.strip()

    return {
        'sha': sha,
        'subject': subject,
        'date': date,
        'message': msg,
        'stat': stat,
        'files_changed': files,
        'diff': diff,
    }

os.chdir('/home/ubuntu/operstor-analysis/operator')

# Conventional fix commits
conventional = []
with open('/home/ubuntu/operstor-analysis/conventional_fixes.txt') as f:
    for line in f:
        parts = line.strip().split('|||')
        if len(parts) >= 3:
            conventional.append(parts[0])

# Non-conventional candidates
nonconv = []
with open('/home/ubuntu/operstor-analysis/candidate_nonconv_fixes.txt') as f:
    for line in f:
        parts = line.strip().split('|||')
        if len(parts) >= 3:
            nonconv.append(parts[0])

print(f"Processing {len(conventional)} conventional fixes...")
all_commits = []
for i, sha in enumerate(conventional):
    try:
        data = get_commit_data(sha)
        data['source'] = 'conventional'
        all_commits.append(data)
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"  Error on {sha}: {e}")
    if (i+1) % 20 == 0:
        print(f"  {i+1}/{len(conventional)} done")

print(f"\nProcessing {len(nonconv)} non-conventional candidates...")
for i, sha in enumerate(nonconv):
    try:
        data = get_commit_data(sha)
        data['source'] = 'nonconventional'
        all_commits.append(data)
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"  Error on {sha}: {e}")
    if (i+1) % 20 == 0:
        print(f"  {i+1}/{len(nonconv)} done")

# Save as JSON
outpath = '/home/ubuntu/operstor-analysis/all_fix_candidates.json'
with open(outpath, 'w') as f:
    json.dump(all_commits, f, indent=2)

print(f"\nSaved {len(all_commits)} commits to {outpath}")

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Generate per-commit bug fix summary markdown files.
This script does the mechanical extraction - categorization and diff analysis.
"""
import json
import os
import re

def classify_bug_area(files_changed, subject, diff):
    """Classify what area of the codebase the bug is in."""
    areas = set()
    files = files_changed.split('\n') if files_changed else []

    for f in files:
        f = f.strip()
        if not f:
            continue
        if 'pebble' in f.lower():
            areas.add('pebble')
        if 'testing' in f.lower() or 'harness' in f.lower() or 'scenario' in f.lower():
            areas.add('testing')
        if 'model' in f.lower():
            areas.add('model')
        if 'framework' in f.lower():
            areas.add('framework')
        if 'storage' in f.lower():
            areas.add('storage')
        if 'main' in f.lower() and 'ops/' in f:
            areas.add('main/dispatch')
        if 'charm' in f.lower():
            areas.add('charm')
        if 'jujucontext' in f.lower() or '_juju_context' in f.lower():
            areas.add('juju-context')
        if 'secret' in f.lower():
            areas.add('secrets')
        if 'relation' in f.lower():
            areas.add('relations')
        if 'log' in f.lower():
            areas.add('logging')
        if f.endswith('.yaml') or f.endswith('.yml'):
            areas.add('ci/config')
        if 'setup.' in f or 'pyproject' in f:
            areas.add('packaging')

    # Subject-based hints
    subj_lower = subject.lower()
    if 'secret' in subj_lower:
        areas.add('secrets')
    if 'relation' in subj_lower:
        areas.add('relations')
    if 'pebble' in subj_lower or 'container' in subj_lower:
        areas.add('pebble')
    if 'storage' in subj_lower:
        areas.add('storage')
    if 'harness' in subj_lower or 'testing' in subj_lower:
        areas.add('testing')
    if 'action' in subj_lower:
        areas.add('actions')
    if 'config' in subj_lower:
        areas.add('config')
    if 'status' in subj_lower:
        areas.add('status')
    if 'network' in subj_lower or 'binding' in subj_lower:
        areas.add('networking')
    if 'security' in subj_lower or 'sqlite' in subj_lower or 'permission' in subj_lower:
        areas.add('security')
    if 'type' in subj_lower and ('annotation' in subj_lower or 'hint' in subj_lower):
        areas.add('type-annotations')

    return sorted(areas) if areas else ['general']


def classify_bug_type(subject, diff, message):
    """Classify the type of bug."""
    types = set()
    combined = (subject + ' ' + message).lower()

    if any(w in combined for w in ['keyerror', 'key error', 'attributeerror', 'attribute error',
                                     'typeerror', 'type error', 'nameerror', 'indexerror']):
        types.add('exception-handling')
    if any(w in combined for w in ['crash', 'traceback', 'unhandled']):
        types.add('crash')
    if any(w in combined for w in ['type annotation', 'type hint', 'return type', 'mypy', 'pyright']):
        types.add('type-annotation')
    if any(w in combined for w in ['race', 'concurrent', 'deadlock', 'threading']):
        types.add('concurrency')
    if any(w in combined for w in ['security', 'permission', 'injection', 'sanitiz', 'escap', 'credential']):
        types.add('security')
    if any(w in combined for w in ['memory', 'leak', 'resource']):
        types.add('resource-leak')
    if any(w in combined for w in ['compat', 'python 3.', 'juju version', 'backward']):
        types.add('compatibility')
    if any(w in combined for w in ['wrong value', 'incorrect', 'wrong result', 'returns wrong']):
        types.add('incorrect-result')
    if any(w in combined for w in ['cache', 'stale', 'invalidat']):
        types.add('caching')
    if any(w in combined for w in ['missing', 'not found', 'undefined']):
        types.add('missing-handling')
    if any(w in combined for w in ['immutab', 'mutabl', 'copy', 'shallow', 'deep copy']):
        types.add('mutability')
    if any(w in combined for w in ['parsing', 'parse', 'format', 'datetime', 'rfc3339']):
        types.add('parsing')
    if 'None' in diff and ('is None' in diff or 'is not None' in diff or '!= None' in diff):
        types.add('null-handling')

    return sorted(types) if types else ['logic-error']


def classify_severity(subject, diff, message):
    """Estimate severity."""
    combined = (subject + ' ' + message).lower()

    if any(w in combined for w in ['security', 'permission', 'injection', 'credential', 'sqlite']):
        return 'high'
    if any(w in combined for w in ['crash', 'startup', 'dispatch', 'unhandled exception', 'traceback']):
        return 'high'
    if any(w in combined for w in ['data loss', 'corrupt', 'silent']):
        return 'high'
    if any(w in combined for w in ['keyerror', 'typeerror', 'attributeerror']):
        return 'medium'
    if any(w in combined for w in ['wrong', 'incorrect', 'broken']):
        return 'medium'
    if any(w in combined for w in ['type annotation', 'type hint', 'docstring', 'typo', 'doc ']):
        return 'low'
    return 'medium'


def is_actual_bugfix(commit):
    """Filter out non-bugfix commits from the candidates."""
    subject = commit['subject'].lower()
    files = commit['files_changed']
    diff = commit['diff']

    # Skip if conventional and already marked as fix
    if commit['source'] == 'conventional':
        return True

    # Filter out obvious non-fixes
    skip_patterns = [
        r'bump version', r'release', r'changelog',
        r'merge (pull|branch|remote)', r'fix merge',
        r'fix ci\b', r'fix lint', r'fix fmt', r'fix tox',
        r'fix test\b', r'fixed test', r'fix utest', r'fixed utest',
        r'fixed itest', r'fix badge', r'fixed badge',
        r'doc fix', r'docs fix', r'fix doc', r'minor doc',
        r'spelling fix', r'typo', r'docstring',
        r'fixed spacing', r'fix spacing', r'missing header',
        r'fixed link', r'fixing.*link',
        r'static fixes',  # static analysis fixes
        r'fix.*example',  # doc example fixes
    ]

    for pat in skip_patterns:
        if re.search(pat, subject):
            # But still include if it touches actual source code significantly
            py_files = [f for f in files.split('\n') if f.strip().endswith('.py') and
                       f.strip().startswith('ops/') and 'test' not in f.lower()]
            if not py_files and not re.search(r'fix.*example', subject):
                return False
            # For some patterns, skip regardless
            if re.search(r'(bump version|release|changelog|merge|fix ci|fix lint|fix fmt|fix tox|doc fix|docs fix|spelling|fixed spacing|fix spacing|missing header|fixed link|fixing.*link|static fixes|fixed badge|fix badge)', subject):
                return False

    # Must touch at least one .py file
    py_files = [f for f in files.split('\n') if f.strip().endswith('.py')]
    if not py_files:
        return False

    # Must have some actual code diff
    if len(diff.strip()) < 50:
        return False

    return True


def summarize_diff(diff):
    """Extract key changes from a diff."""
    lines = diff.split('\n')
    added = [l[1:] for l in lines if l.startswith('+') and not l.startswith('+++')]
    removed = [l[1:] for l in lines if l.startswith('-') and not l.startswith('---')]

    # Count meaningful changes (skip blank lines and comments)
    meaningful_added = [l for l in added if l.strip() and not l.strip().startswith('#')]
    meaningful_removed = [l for l in removed if l.strip() and not l.strip().startswith('#')]

    return {
        'lines_added': len(added),
        'lines_removed': len(removed),
        'meaningful_added': len(meaningful_added),
        'meaningful_removed': len(meaningful_removed),
    }


def main():
    with open('/home/ubuntu/operstor-analysis/all_fix_candidates.json') as f:
        commits = json.load(f)

    outdir = '/home/ubuntu/operstor-analysis/commit-summaries'
    os.makedirs(outdir, exist_ok=True)

    included = []
    excluded = []

    for commit in commits:
        if not is_actual_bugfix(commit):
            excluded.append(commit)
            continue

        areas = classify_bug_area(commit['files_changed'], commit['subject'], commit['diff'])
        bug_types = classify_bug_type(commit['subject'], commit['diff'], commit['message'])
        severity = classify_severity(commit['subject'], commit['diff'], commit['message'])
        diff_stats = summarize_diff(commit['diff'])

        commit['areas'] = areas
        commit['bug_types'] = bug_types
        commit['severity'] = severity
        commit['diff_stats'] = diff_stats
        included.append(commit)

        # Write individual markdown file
        sha_short = commit['sha'][:8]
        filename = f"{commit['date']}_{sha_short}.md"

        with open(os.path.join(outdir, filename), 'w') as f:
            f.write(f"# {commit['subject']}\n\n")
            f.write(f"- **Commit**: [{commit['sha'][:12]}](https://github.com/canonical/operator/commit/{commit['sha']})\n")
            f.write(f"- **Date**: {commit['date']}\n")
            f.write(f"- **Areas**: {', '.join(areas)}\n")
            f.write(f"- **Bug Types**: {', '.join(bug_types)}\n")
            f.write(f"- **Severity**: {severity}\n")
            f.write(f"- **Lines Changed**: +{diff_stats['lines_added']}/-{diff_stats['lines_removed']}\n")
            f.write(f"- **Source**: {commit['source']}\n\n")

            f.write(f"## Commit Message\n\n")
            f.write(f"```\n{commit['message']}\n```\n\n")

            f.write(f"## Files Changed\n\n")
            for fpath in commit['files_changed'].split('\n'):
                if fpath.strip():
                    f.write(f"- `{fpath.strip()}`\n")
            f.write('\n')

            f.write(f"## Diff\n\n")
            f.write(f"```diff\n{commit['diff'][:20000]}\n```\n")

    # Write index
    with open('/home/ubuntu/operstor-analysis/commit-summaries/INDEX.md', 'w') as f:
        f.write("# Bug Fix Commit Index\n\n")
        f.write(f"Total bug fix commits analyzed: {len(included)}\n\n")

        # Group by severity
        for sev in ['high', 'medium', 'low']:
            sev_commits = [c for c in included if c['severity'] == sev]
            if not sev_commits:
                continue
            f.write(f"## {sev.upper()} Severity ({len(sev_commits)})\n\n")
            f.write("| Date | Commit | Subject | Areas | Bug Types |\n")
            f.write("|------|--------|---------|-------|-----------|\n")
            for c in sorted(sev_commits, key=lambda x: x['date'], reverse=True):
                sha_short = c['sha'][:8]
                link = f"[{sha_short}](https://github.com/canonical/operator/commit/{c['sha']})"
                areas_str = ', '.join(c['areas'])
                types_str = ', '.join(c['bug_types'])
                subj = c['subject'].replace('|', '\\|')
                f.write(f"| {c['date']} | {link} | {subj} | {areas_str} | {types_str} |\n")
            f.write('\n')

    # Save the filtered data for pattern analysis
    with open('/home/ubuntu/operstor-analysis/filtered_fixes.json', 'w') as f:
        json.dump(included, f, indent=2)

    print(f"Included: {len(included)} bug fixes")
    print(f"Excluded: {len(excluded)} non-bug-fix commits")

    # Area distribution
    from collections import Counter
    area_counts = Counter()
    type_counts = Counter()
    sev_counts = Counter()
    for c in included:
        for a in c['areas']:
            area_counts[a] += 1
        for t in c['bug_types']:
            type_counts[t] += 1
        sev_counts[c['severity']] += 1

    print(f"\nSeverity distribution: {dict(sev_counts)}")
    print(f"\nTop areas: {area_counts.most_common(15)}")
    print(f"\nTop bug types: {type_counts.most_common(15)}")


if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Generate per-commit bug fix summary markdown files.
This script does the mechanical extraction - categorization and diff analysis.
"""
import json
import os
import re

def classify_bug_area(files_changed, subject, diff):
    """Classify what area of the codebase the bug is in."""
    areas = set()
    files = files_changed.split('\n') if files_changed else []

    for f in files:
        f = f.strip()
        if not f:
            continue
        if 'pebble' in f.lower():
            areas.add('pebble')
        if 'testing' in f.lower() or 'harness' in f.lower() or 'scenario' in f.lower():
            areas.add('testing')
        if 'model' in f.lower():
            areas.add('model')
        if 'framework' in f.lower():
            areas.add('framework')
        if 'storage' in f.lower():
            areas.add('storage')
        if 'main' in f.lower() and 'ops/' in f:
            areas.add('main/dispatch')
        if 'charm' in f.lower():
            areas.add('charm')
        if 'jujucontext' in f.lower() or '_juju_context' in f.lower():
            areas.add('juju-context')
        if 'secret' in f.lower():
            areas.add('secrets')
        if 'relation' in f.lower():
            areas.add('relations')
        if 'log' in f.lower():
            areas.add('logging')
        if f.endswith('.yaml') or f.endswith('.yml'):
            areas.add('ci/config')
        if 'setup.' in f or 'pyproject' in f:
            areas.add('packaging')

    # Subject-based hints
    subj_lower = subject.lower()
    if 'secret' in subj_lower:
        areas.add('secrets')
    if 'relation' in subj_lower:
        areas.add('relations')
    if 'pebble' in subj_lower or 'container' in subj_lower:
        areas.add('pebble')
    if 'storage' in subj_lower:
        areas.add('storage')
    if 'harness' in subj_lower or 'testing' in subj_lower:
        areas.add('testing')
    if 'action' in subj_lower:
        areas.add('actions')
    if 'config' in subj_lower:
        areas.add('config')
    if 'status' in subj_lower:
        areas.add('status')
    if 'network' in subj_lower or 'binding' in subj_lower:
        areas.add('networking')
    if 'security' in subj_lower or 'sqlite' in subj_lower or 'permission' in subj_lower:
        areas.add('security')
    if 'type' in subj_lower and ('annotation' in subj_lower or 'hint' in subj_lower):
        areas.add('type-annotations')

    return sorted(areas) if areas else ['general']


def classify_bug_type(subject, diff, message):
    """Classify the type of bug."""
    types = set()
    combined = (subject + ' ' + message).lower()

    if any(w in combined for w in ['keyerror', 'key error', 'attributeerror', 'attribute error',
                                     'typeerror', 'type error', 'nameerror', 'indexerror']):
        types.add('exception-handling')
    if any(w in combined for w in ['crash', 'traceback', 'unhandled']):
        types.add('crash')
    if any(w in combined for w in ['type annotation', 'type hint', 'return type', 'mypy', 'pyright']):
        types.add('type-annotation')
    if any(w in combined for w in ['race', 'concurrent', 'deadlock', 'threading']):
        types.add('concurrency')
    if any(w in combined for w in ['security', 'permission', 'injection', 'sanitiz', 'escap', 'credential']):
        types.add('security')
    if any(w in combined for w in ['memory', 'leak', 'resource']):
        types.add('resource-leak')
    if any(w in combined for w in ['compat', 'python 3.', 'juju version', 'backward']):
        types.add('compatibility')
    if any(w in combined for w in ['wrong value', 'incorrect', 'wrong result', 'returns wrong']):
        types.add('incorrect-result')
    if any(w in combined for w in ['cache', 'stale', 'invalidat']):
        types.add('caching')
    if any(w in combined for w in ['missing', 'not found', 'undefined']):
        types.add('missing-handling')
    if any(w in combined for w in ['immutab', 'mutabl', 'copy', 'shallow', 'deep copy']):
        types.add('mutability')
    if any(w in combined for w in ['parsing', 'parse', 'format', 'datetime', 'rfc3339']):
        types.add('parsing')
    if 'None' in diff and ('is None' in diff or 'is not None' in diff or '!= None' in diff):
        types.add('null-handling')

    return sorted(types) if types else ['logic-error']


def classify_severity(subject, diff, message):
    """Estimate severity."""
    combined = (subject + ' ' + message).lower()

    if any(w in combined for w in ['security', 'permission', 'injection', 'credential', 'sqlite']):
        return 'high'
    if any(w in combined for w in ['crash', 'startup', 'dispatch', 'unhandled exception', 'traceback']):
        return 'high'
    if any(w in combined for w in ['data loss', 'corrupt', 'silent']):
        return 'high'
    if any(w in combined for w in ['keyerror', 'typeerror', 'attributeerror']):
        return 'medium'
    if any(w in combined for w in ['wrong', 'incorrect', 'broken']):
        return 'medium'
    if any(w in combined for w in ['type annotation', 'type hint', 'docstring', 'typo', 'doc ']):
        return 'low'
    return 'medium'


def is_actual_bugfix(commit):
    """Filter out non-bugfix commits from the candidates."""
    subject = commit['subject'].lower()
    files = commit['files_changed']
    diff = commit['diff']

    # Skip if conventional and already marked as fix
    if commit['source'] == 'conventional':
        return True

    # Filter out obvious non-fixes
    skip_patterns = [
        r'bump version', r'release', r'changelog',
        r'merge (pull|branch|remote)', r'fix merge',
        r'fix ci\b', r'fix lint', r'fix fmt', r'fix tox',
        r'fix test\b', r'fixed test', r'fix utest', r'fixed utest',
        r'fixed itest', r'fix badge', r'fixed badge',
        r'doc fix', r'docs fix', r'fix doc', r'minor doc',
        r'spelling fix', r'typo', r'docstring',
        r'fixed spacing', r'fix spacing', r'missing header',
        r'fixed link', r'fixing.*link',
        r'static fixes',  # static analysis fixes
        r'fix.*example',  # doc example fixes
    ]

    for pat in skip_patterns:
        if re.search(pat, subject):
            # But still include if it touches actual source code significantly
            py_files = [f for f in files.split('\n') if f.strip().endswith('.py') and
                       f.strip().startswith('ops/') and 'test' not in f.lower()]
            if not py_files and not re.search(r'fix.*example', subject):
                return False
            # For some patterns, skip regardless
            if re.search(r'(bump version|release|changelog|merge|fix ci|fix lint|fix fmt|fix tox|doc fix|docs fix|spelling|fixed spacing|fix spacing|missing header|fixed link|fixing.*link|static fixes|fixed badge|fix badge)', subject):
                return False

    # Must touch at least one .py file
    py_files = [f for f in files.split('\n') if f.strip().endswith('.py')]
    if not py_files:
        return False

    # Must have some actual code diff
    if len(diff.strip()) < 50:
        return False

    return True


def summarize_diff(diff):
    """Extract key changes from a diff."""
    lines = diff.split('\n')
    added = [l[1:] for l in lines if l.startswith('+') and not l.startswith('+++')]
    removed = [l[1:] for l in lines if l.startswith('-') and not l.startswith('---')]

    # Count meaningful changes (skip blank lines and comments)
    meaningful_added = [l for l in added if l.strip() and not l.strip().startswith('#')]
    meaningful_removed = [l for l in removed if l.strip() and not l.strip().startswith('#')]

    return {
        'lines_added': len(added),
        'lines_removed': len(removed),
        'meaningful_added': len(meaningful_added),
        'meaningful_removed': len(meaningful_removed),
    }


def main():
    with open('/home/ubuntu/operstor-analysis/all_fix_candidates.json') as f:
        commits = json.load(f)

    outdir = '/home/ubuntu/operstor-analysis/commit-summaries'
    os.makedirs(outdir, exist_ok=True)

    included = []
    excluded = []

    for commit in commits:
        if not is_actual_bugfix(commit):
            excluded.append(commit)
            continue

        areas = classify_bug_area(commit['files_changed'], commit['subject'], commit['diff'])
        bug_types = classify_bug_type(commit['subject'], commit['diff'], commit['message'])
        severity = classify_severity(commit['subject'], commit['diff'], commit['message'])
        diff_stats = summarize_diff(commit['diff'])

        commit['areas'] = areas
        commit['bug_types'] = bug_types
        commit['severity'] = severity
        commit['diff_stats'] = diff_stats
        included.append(commit)

        # Write individual markdown file
        sha_short = commit['sha'][:8]
        filename = f"{commit['date']}_{sha_short}.md"

        with open(os.path.join(outdir, filename), 'w') as f:
            f.write(f"# {commit['subject']}\n\n")
            f.write(f"- **Commit**: [{commit['sha'][:12]}](https://github.com/canonical/operator/commit/{commit['sha']})\n")
            f.write(f"- **Date**: {commit['date']}\n")
            f.write(f"- **Areas**: {', '.join(areas)}\n")
            f.write(f"- **Bug Types**: {', '.join(bug_types)}\n")
            f.write(f"- **Severity**: {severity}\n")
            f.write(f"- **Lines Changed**: +{diff_stats['lines_added']}/-{diff_stats['lines_removed']}\n")
            f.write(f"- **Source**: {commit['source']}\n\n")

            f.write(f"## Commit Message\n\n")
            f.write(f"```\n{commit['message']}\n```\n\n")

            f.write(f"## Files Changed\n\n")
            for fpath in commit['files_changed'].split('\n'):
                if fpath.strip():
                    f.write(f"- `{fpath.strip()}`\n")
            f.write('\n')

            f.write(f"## Diff\n\n")
            f.write(f"```diff\n{commit['diff'][:20000]}\n```\n")

    # Write index
    with open('/home/ubuntu/operstor-analysis/commit-summaries/INDEX.md', 'w') as f:
        f.write("# Bug Fix Commit Index\n\n")
        f.write(f"Total bug fix commits analyzed: {len(included)}\n\n")

        # Group by severity
        for sev in ['high', 'medium', 'low']:
            sev_commits = [c for c in included if c['severity'] == sev]
            if not sev_commits:
                continue
            f.write(f"## {sev.upper()} Severity ({len(sev_commits)})\n\n")
            f.write("| Date | Commit | Subject | Areas | Bug Types |\n")
            f.write("|------|--------|---------|-------|-----------|\n")
            for c in sorted(sev_commits, key=lambda x: x['date'], reverse=True):
                sha_short = c['sha'][:8]
                link = f"[{sha_short}](https://github.com/canonical/operator/commit/{c['sha']})"
                areas_str = ', '.join(c['areas'])
                types_str = ', '.join(c['bug_types'])
                subj = c['subject'].replace('|', '\\|')
                f.write(f"| {c['date']} | {link} | {subj} | {areas_str} | {types_str} |\n")
            f.write('\n')

    # Save the filtered data for pattern analysis
    with open('/home/ubuntu/operstor-analysis/filtered_fixes.json', 'w') as f:
        json.dump(included, f, indent=2)

    print(f"Included: {len(included)} bug fixes")
    print(f"Excluded: {len(excluded)} non-bug-fix commits")

    # Area distribution
    from collections import Counter
    area_counts = Counter()
    type_counts = Counter()
    sev_counts = Counter()
    for c in included:
        for a in c['areas']:
            area_counts[a] += 1
        for t in c['bug_types']:
            type_counts[t] += 1
        sev_counts[c['severity']] += 1

    print(f"\nSeverity distribution: {dict(sev_counts)}")
    print(f"\nTop areas: {area_counts.most_common(15)}")
    print(f"\nTop bug types: {type_counts.most_common(15)}")


if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Generate per-commit bug fix summary markdown files.
This script does the mechanical extraction - categorization and diff analysis.
"""
import json
import os
import re

def classify_bug_area(files_changed, subject, diff):
    """Classify what area of the codebase the bug is in."""
    areas = set()
    files = files_changed.split('\n') if files_changed else []

    for f in files:
        f = f.strip()
        if not f:
            continue
        if 'pebble' in f.lower():
            areas.add('pebble')
        if 'testing' in f.lower() or 'harness' in f.lower() or 'scenario' in f.lower():
            areas.add('testing')
        if 'model' in f.lower():
            areas.add('model')
        if 'framework' in f.lower():
            areas.add('framework')
        if 'storage' in f.lower():
            areas.add('storage')
        if 'main' in f.lower() and 'ops/' in f:
            areas.add('main/dispatch')
        if 'charm' in f.lower():
            areas.add('charm')
        if 'jujucontext' in f.lower() or '_juju_context' in f.lower():
            areas.add('juju-context')
        if 'secret' in f.lower():
            areas.add('secrets')
        if 'relation' in f.lower():
            areas.add('relations')
        if 'log' in f.lower():
            areas.add('logging')
        if f.endswith('.yaml') or f.endswith('.yml'):
            areas.add('ci/config')
        if 'setup.' in f or 'pyproject' in f:
            areas.add('packaging')

    # Subject-based hints
    subj_lower = subject.lower()
    if 'secret' in subj_lower:
        areas.add('secrets')
    if 'relation' in subj_lower:
        areas.add('relations')
    if 'pebble' in subj_lower or 'container' in subj_lower:
        areas.add('pebble')
    if 'storage' in subj_lower:
        areas.add('storage')
    if 'harness' in subj_lower or 'testing' in subj_lower:
        areas.add('testing')
    if 'action' in subj_lower:
        areas.add('actions')
    if 'config' in subj_lower:
        areas.add('config')
    if 'status' in subj_lower:
        areas.add('status')
    if 'network' in subj_lower or 'binding' in subj_lower:
        areas.add('networking')
    if 'security' in subj_lower or 'sqlite' in subj_lower or 'permission' in subj_lower:
        areas.add('security')
    if 'type' in subj_lower and ('annotation' in subj_lower or 'hint' in subj_lower):
        areas.add('type-annotations')

    return sorted(areas) if areas else ['general']


def classify_bug_type(subject, diff, message):
    """Classify the type of bug."""
    types = set()
    combined = (subject + ' ' + message).lower()

    if any(w in combined for w in ['keyerror', 'key error', 'attributeerror', 'attribute error',
                                     'typeerror', 'type error', 'nameerror', 'indexerror']):
        types.add('exception-handling')
    if any(w in combined for w in ['crash', 'traceback', 'unhandled']):
        types.add('crash')
    if any(w in combined for w in ['type annotation', 'type hint', 'return type', 'mypy', 'pyright']):
        types.add('type-annotation')
    if any(w in combined for w in ['race', 'concurrent', 'deadlock', 'threading']):
        types.add('concurrency')
    if any(w in combined for w in ['security', 'permission', 'injection', 'sanitiz', 'escap', 'credential']):
        types.add('security')
    if any(w in combined for w in ['memory', 'leak', 'resource']):
        types.add('resource-leak')
    if any(w in combined for w in ['compat', 'python 3.', 'juju version', 'backward']):
        types.add('compatibility')
    if any(w in combined for w in ['wrong value', 'incorrect', 'wrong result', 'returns wrong']):
        types.add('incorrect-result')
    if any(w in combined for w in ['cache', 'stale', 'invalidat']):
        types.add('caching')
    if any(w in combined for w in ['missing', 'not found', 'undefined']):
        types.add('missing-handling')
    if any(w in combined for w in ['immutab', 'mutabl', 'copy', 'shallow', 'deep copy']):
        types.add('mutability')
    if any(w in combined for w in ['parsing', 'parse', 'format', 'datetime', 'rfc3339']):
        types.add('parsing')
    if 'None' in diff and ('is None' in diff or 'is not None' in diff or '!= None' in diff):
        types.add('null-handling')

    return sorted(types) if types else ['logic-error']


def classify_severity(subject, diff, message):
    """Estimate severity."""
    combined = (subject + ' ' + message).lower()

    if any(w in combined for w in ['security', 'permission', 'injection', 'credential', 'sqlite']):
        return 'high'
    if any(w in combined for w in ['crash', 'startup', 'dispatch', 'unhandled exception', 'traceback']):
        return 'high'
    if any(w in combined for w in ['data loss', 'corrupt', 'silent']):
        return 'high'
    if any(w in combined for w in ['keyerror', 'typeerror', 'attributeerror']):
        return 'medium'
    if any(w in combined for w in ['wrong', 'incorrect', 'broken']):
        return 'medium'
    if any(w in combined for w in ['type annotation', 'type hint', 'docstring', 'typo', 'doc ']):
        return 'low'
    return 'medium'


def is_actual_bugfix(commit):
    """Filter out non-bugfix commits from the candidates."""
    subject = commit['subject'].lower()
    files = commit['files_changed']
    diff = commit['diff']

    # Skip if conventional and already marked as fix
    if commit['source'] == 'conventional':
        return True

    # Filter out obvious non-fixes
    skip_patterns = [
        r'bump version', r'release', r'changelog',
        r'merge (pull|branch|remote)', r'fix merge',
        r'fix ci\b', r'fix lint', r'fix fmt', r'fix tox',
        r'fix test\b', r'fixed test', r'fix utest', r'fixed utest',
        r'fixed itest', r'fix badge', r'fixed badge',
        r'doc fix', r'docs fix', r'fix doc', r'minor doc',
        r'spelling fix', r'typo', r'docstring',
        r'fixed spacing', r'fix spacing', r'missing header',
        r'fixed link', r'fixing.*link',
        r'static fixes',  # static analysis fixes
        r'fix.*example',  # doc example fixes
    ]

    for pat in skip_patterns:
        if re.search(pat, subject):
            # But still include if it touches actual source code significantly
            py_files = [f for f in files.split('\n') if f.strip().endswith('.py') and
                       f.strip().startswith('ops/') and 'test' not in f.lower()]
            if not py_files and not re.search(r'fix.*example', subject):
                return False
            # For some patterns, skip regardless
            if re.search(r'(bump version|release|changelog|merge|fix ci|fix lint|fix fmt|fix tox|doc fix|docs fix|spelling|fixed spacing|fix spacing|missing header|fixed link|fixing.*link|static fixes|fixed badge|fix badge)', subject):
                return False

    # Must touch at least one .py file
    py_files = [f for f in files.split('\n') if f.strip().endswith('.py')]
    if not py_files:
        return False

    # Must have some actual code diff
    if len(diff.strip()) < 50:
        return False

    return True


def summarize_diff(diff):
    """Extract key changes from a diff."""
    lines = diff.split('\n')
    added = [l[1:] for l in lines if l.startswith('+') and not l.startswith('+++')]
    removed = [l[1:] for l in lines if l.startswith('-') and not l.startswith('---')]

    # Count meaningful changes (skip blank lines and comments)
    meaningful_added = [l for l in added if l.strip() and not l.strip().startswith('#')]
    meaningful_removed = [l for l in removed if l.strip() and not l.strip().startswith('#')]

    return {
        'lines_added': len(added),
        'lines_removed': len(removed),
        'meaningful_added': len(meaningful_added),
        'meaningful_removed': len(meaningful_removed),
    }


def main():
    with open('/home/ubuntu/operstor-analysis/all_fix_candidates.json') as f:
        commits = json.load(f)

    outdir = '/home/ubuntu/operstor-analysis/commit-summaries'
    os.makedirs(outdir, exist_ok=True)

    included = []
    excluded = []

    for commit in commits:
        if not is_actual_bugfix(commit):
            excluded.append(commit)
            continue

        areas = classify_bug_area(commit['files_changed'], commit['subject'], commit['diff'])
        bug_types = classify_bug_type(commit['subject'], commit['diff'], commit['message'])
        severity = classify_severity(commit['subject'], commit['diff'], commit['message'])
        diff_stats = summarize_diff(commit['diff'])

        commit['areas'] = areas
        commit['bug_types'] = bug_types
        commit['severity'] = severity
        commit['diff_stats'] = diff_stats
        included.append(commit)

        # Write individual markdown file
        sha_short = commit['sha'][:8]
        filename = f"{commit['date']}_{sha_short}.md"

        with open(os.path.join(outdir, filename), 'w') as f:
            f.write(f"# {commit['subject']}\n\n")
            f.write(f"- **Commit**: [{commit['sha'][:12]}](https://github.com/canonical/operator/commit/{commit['sha']})\n")
            f.write(f"- **Date**: {commit['date']}\n")
            f.write(f"- **Areas**: {', '.join(areas)}\n")
            f.write(f"- **Bug Types**: {', '.join(bug_types)}\n")
            f.write(f"- **Severity**: {severity}\n")
            f.write(f"- **Lines Changed**: +{diff_stats['lines_added']}/-{diff_stats['lines_removed']}\n")
            f.write(f"- **Source**: {commit['source']}\n\n")

            f.write(f"## Commit Message\n\n")
            f.write(f"```\n{commit['message']}\n```\n\n")

            f.write(f"## Files Changed\n\n")
            for fpath in commit['files_changed'].split('\n'):
                if fpath.strip():
                    f.write(f"- `{fpath.strip()}`\n")
            f.write('\n')

            f.write(f"## Diff\n\n")
            f.write(f"```diff\n{commit['diff'][:20000]}\n```\n")

    # Write index
    with open('/home/ubuntu/operstor-analysis/commit-summaries/INDEX.md', 'w') as f:
        f.write("# Bug Fix Commit Index\n\n")
        f.write(f"Total bug fix commits analyzed: {len(included)}\n\n")

        # Group by severity
        for sev in ['high', 'medium', 'low']:
            sev_commits = [c for c in included if c['severity'] == sev]
            if not sev_commits:
                continue
            f.write(f"## {sev.upper()} Severity ({len(sev_commits)})\n\n")
            f.write("| Date | Commit | Subject | Areas | Bug Types |\n")
            f.write("|------|--------|---------|-------|-----------|\n")
            for c in sorted(sev_commits, key=lambda x: x['date'], reverse=True):
                sha_short = c['sha'][:8]
                link = f"[{sha_short}](https://github.com/canonical/operator/commit/{c['sha']})"
                areas_str = ', '.join(c['areas'])
                types_str = ', '.join(c['bug_types'])
                subj = c['subject'].replace('|', '\\|')
                f.write(f"| {c['date']} | {link} | {subj} | {areas_str} | {types_str} |\n")
            f.write('\n')

    # Save the filtered data for pattern analysis
    with open('/home/ubuntu/operstor-analysis/filtered_fixes.json', 'w') as f:
        json.dump(included, f, indent=2)

    print(f"Included: {len(included)} bug fixes")
    print(f"Excluded: {len(excluded)} non-bug-fix commits")

    # Area distribution
    from collections import Counter
    area_counts = Counter()
    type_counts = Counter()
    sev_counts = Counter()
    for c in included:
        for a in c['areas']:
            area_counts[a] += 1
        for t in c['bug_types']:
            type_counts[t] += 1
        sev_counts[c['severity']] += 1

    print(f"\nSeverity distribution: {dict(sev_counts)}")
    print(f"\nTop areas: {area_counts.most_common(15)}")
    print(f"\nTop bug types: {type_counts.most_common(15)}")


if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()

You are analyzing bug fixes in the canonical/operator repository. The repo is cloned at /home/ubuntu/operstor-analysis/operator.

Your job: Read through the bug fix commit diffs for the most important fixes and produce a detailed analysis JSON file. Focus on:
1. What exactly was the bug (root cause)
2. What was the fix
3. What pattern does this represent (e.g., "forgotten None check", "mutability of shared dict", "wrong API call format")
4. Could this class of bug recur?

The commit summaries are in /home/ubuntu/operstor-analysis/commit-summaries/ as individual .md files.
The filtered data is at /home/ubuntu/operstor-analysis/filtered_fixes.json (without diffs) and /home/ubuntu/operstor-analysis/filtered_fixes_full.json (with diffs).

Process ALL 15 high severity fixes and a representative sample of ~50 medium severity fixes (pick ones from different areas and bug types for good coverage). For each, read the commit diff using `git show <sha> -- '*.py'` in the operator repo directory.

Write your analysis to /home/ubuntu/operstor-analysis/detailed_analysis.json with this structure:
```json
[
  {
    "sha": "...",
    "subject": "...",
    "date": "...",
    "severity": "...",
    "root_cause": "one paragraph explaining the root cause",
    "fix_description": "one paragraph explaining the fix",
    "pattern": "short pattern name",
    "pattern_description": "description of the general pattern this represents",
    "recurrence_risk": "high/medium/low",
    "areas": [...],
    "security_relevant": true/false,
    "could_cause_crash": true/false,
    "could_cause_data_loss": true/false,
    "lesson": "one sentence lesson for developers"
  }
]
```

Work through this methodically. Read the actual diffs — don't guess from titles alone.

Your job: Read through the bug fix commit diffs for the remaining fixes (low severity and medium severity ones not covered by the other agent) and produce an analysis JSON file.

The commit summaries are in /home/ubuntu/operstor-analysis/commit-summaries/ as individual .md files.
The filtered data is at /home/ubuntu/operstor-analysis/filtered_fixes.json (without diffs).

Process ALL 36 low severity fixes and ~50 medium severity fixes. For each, read the commit diff using `git show <sha> -- '*.py'` (and also without the -- filter for CI/docs fixes) in the /home/ubuntu/operstor-analysis/operator directory.

Focus on:
1. What was the bug (root cause)
2. What was the fix
3. What pattern does this represent
4. Category: is this a test fix, CI fix, docs fix, build fix, or source fix?

Write your analysis to /home/ubuntu/operstor-analysis/detailed_analysis_remaining.json with this structure:
```json
[
  {
    "sha": "...",
    "subject": "...",
    "date": "...",
    "severity": "...",
    "root_cause": "one paragraph explaining the root cause",
    "fix_description": "one paragraph explaining the fix",
    "pattern": "short pattern name",
    "pattern_description": "description of the general pattern",
    "fix_category": "source-fix|test-fix|docs-fix|ci-fix|build-fix",
    "areas": [...],
    "lesson": "one sentence lesson for developers"
  }
]
```

Work through this methodically. Read the actual diffs — don't guess from titles alone.

content.text:
I've launched two agents in the background to analyze all the commit diffs in detail — one handling high/medium severity fixes, the other handling low/remaining fixes. They'll read the actual diffs via `git show` and produce structured analysis.

While those run, let me start exploring the current codebase to prepare for the code audit (deliverable 3). I'll look at the structure and key source files.

<result>Now I'll compile my comprehensive analysis:

## Comprehensive Codebase Architecture & Pattern Analysis

### Overview
The canonical/operator codebase (~20,500 lines) is a sophisticated Python framework for writing Juju charms. It provides a high-level API for charm developers to interact with the Juju runtime while abstracting away complex hook command interactions. The architecture follows clear separation of concerns across five main layers: framework (event handling), model (state management), storage (persistence), charm (lifecycle), and hookcmds (Juju integration).

---

## 1. DATA SAFETY & PASSING PATTERNS

### 1.1 Data Copying Strategies

**Defensive Copying:**
- **Secret Content**: `model.py:1475` - `get_content()` returns `self._content.copy()`, creating a shallow copy before returning to prevent external mutations of cached secret data
- **Relation Data**: `model.py:1826` - Uses `copy.deepcopy(kwargs)` in `RelationDataContent.from_dict()` for configuration unpacking, ensuring isolation of deserialized data
- **Pebble Configuration**: `pebble.py:1115-1126` - Check and LogTarget objects deep copy nested dictionaries (`http`, `tcp`, `exec_`, `labels`) during initialization to prevent mutation of input dicts

**Array/List Copying:**
- **Relation Unit Lists**: `model.py:1266` - Pebble services list uses `list(dct.get('services', []))` to create a new list rather than sharing the reference
- **Command Arguments**: `_main.py:193` - Dispatcher copies `sys.argv` with `.copy()` before modifying for legacy hook execution

**Shallow vs Deep Copy Patterns:**
- Shallow copies used for simple dicts: relation data, relation updates
- Deep copies used when nested structures present: pebble configs, relation data with JSON encoding

### 1.2 Dictionary/Collection Handling

**Immutability Through Freezing:**
- `testing/state.py:130-140` - `_deepcopy_mutable_fields()` handles the fact that frozen dataclasses can't actually be immutable on nested collections, so it replaces dict/list references with deep copies to disassociate from the original object
- **Rationale noted**: Full frozendict not used to maintain backward compatibility with tests that expect mutable lists

**Lazy Loading Pattern:**
- `model.py:861-906` - `_GenericLazyMapping` and `LazyMapping` defer dict population until first access, with `_invalidate()` method to clear cached data
- Used for relation data, configuration, relation lists to avoid unnecessary Juju queries

**Read-Only Mappings:**
- `model.py:909-960` - `RelationMapping` implements `Mapping` interface (read-only), with underlying `_cache` dict and `_invalidate()` methods for cache invalidation

### 1.3 Snapshot/Restore Serialization

**Pickle-Based Persistence:**
- `storage.py:21, 135, 151` - Uses `pickle.dumps()` and `pickle.loads()` for object snapshots in SQLiteStorage
- Noted limitation in comments: `storage.py:120-124` - No rollback support; if process dies during snapshot operations, system must handle recovery

**YAML Safe Handling:**
- `storage.py:331-366` - Custom `_SimpleLoader` and `_SimpleDumper` that only support basic Python types and tuples
- Avoids pickle's unsafe arbitrary type deserialization for Juju-side state

---

## 2. ERROR HANDLING PATTERNS

### 2.1 Exception Handling Architecture

**Layered Exception Raising:**
- `_main.py:247-252` - Custom `_Abort` exception for system-level failures (not for charm logic)
- `storage.py:423-430` - `NoSnapshotError` with custom message formatting for clarity
- `model.py:3422-3440` - Specific exception hierarchy: `RelationDataError`, `RelationDataTypeError`, `RelationDataAccessError`, `SecretNotFoundError`, `RelationNotFoundError`

**Try-Catch Patterns:**
- `_main.py:196-206` - Subprocess error handling with detailed logging of exit codes and stderr
- `storage.py:81-91` - File operation error wrapping: catch `OSError` and re-raise as `RuntimeError` with context
- `jujucontext.py:237-393` - Comprehensive validation of environment variables with clear `ValueError` messages for each missing required var

### 2.2 Context Manager Safety

**Resource Cleanup:**
- `_main.py:297-299, 484-486` - Tracing context manager explicitly entered/exited with `__enter__`/`__exit__` and exception info propagation
- `hookcmds/_secret.py:64-69, 303-308` - Temporary directory context managers for secret files with automatic cleanup
- `model.py:3709-3724` - Pod spec file handling with explicit tmpdir cleanup in try-finally block

**Harness Testing Context:**
- `testing/context.py:105-138` - Manager context manager with safeguards against double-emit via `_emitted` flag and explicit error on improper usage

### 2.3 Edge Case Handling

**None/Empty Value Checks:**
- `storage.py:318-320` - Notice list loads check for both `None` and missing key scenarios
- `jujucontext.py:244-247, 282-285` - Fallback to defaults when JUJU_* vars are empty or missing
- `model.py:1473-1474` - Conditional refresh of secret content based on cache state

**Bounded Iteration:**
- `storage.py:165-170, 216-221` - Generator-based snapshot/notice iteration with `fetchmany()` for memory efficiency (avoid loading all rows)

---

## 3. SECURITY-RELEVANT PATTERNS

### 3.1 File Permissions

**Database File Security:**
- `storage.py:77-91` - Explicit `_ensure_db_permissions()` sets restrictive `stat.S_IRUSR | stat.S_IWUSR` (0o600) on SQLite files
- Uses `os.open()` with `O_CREAT | O_EXCL` and explicit mode for atomic creation with proper permissions
- Applies `chmod()` after file exists to handle edge cases

**Command Execution:**
- `storage.py:49-54` - `_run()` function uses `shutil.which()` to locate command before execution (prevents PATH injection)
- `_main.py:49-52` - `_exe_path()` function searches charm directory explicitly for legacy hooks

### 3.2 Credential/Secret Handling

**Secret Content in Temporary Files:**
- `hookcmds/_secret.py:63-69, 302-308` - Secret content written to individual temp files with full paths rather than command-line arguments
- Prevents credential leakage via process environment or ps output
- Files cleaned up automatically by context manager

**Secret Caching:**
- `model.py:1473-1474` - Secret content cached in memory after first fetch; `peek_content()` exists for non-cached access

### 3.3 Command Injection Prevention

**No Shell Execution:**
- All subprocess calls use list-form arguments: `subprocess.run([cmd, *args])` not string interpolation
- `hookcmds/_utils.py:49-50` - Uses `capture_output=True, check=True, encoding='utf-8'`
- `_main.py:197` - `subprocess.run(argv, check=True)` where argv is a list

**YAML Safe Deserialization:**
- `storage.py:410` - Uses custom `_SimpleLoader` (commented as `# noqa: S506` but uses safe loader)
- `hookcmds/_relation.py:81-86` - JSON parsing with `json.loads()` for relation data

### 3.4 Environment Variable Validation

**Strict Validation:**
- `jujucontext.py:237-392` - Every required env var explicitly checked and validated
- Type conversions with validation: `int(env['JUJU_RELATION_ID'].split(':')[-1])`
- Event-specific required vars validated based on hook type

---

## 4. COMPATIBILITY & VERSION PATTERNS

### 4.1 Version Checking

**JujuVersion Class with Feature Flags:**
- `jujuversion.py:26-176` - Version tuple comparison `(major, minor, patch) >= (X, Y, Z)`
- Methods: `has_app_data()`, `has_controller_storage()`, `has_secrets`, `supports_open_port_on_k8s()`, `supports_exec_service_context()`, `supports_pebble_log_forwarding()`, `supports_pebble_identities()`, `supports_pebble_metrics()`

**Storage Backend Selection:**
- `_main.py:224-244` - `_should_use_controller_storage()` checks Juju version and charm metadata
- Falls back to local SQLite storage if version too old or not a podspec charm
- Logs decision with version number for debugging

**Deprecated Features:**
- `_main.py:182, 348-352` - Warnings for legacy hooks (`DeprecationWarning`) and controller storage (`DeprecationWarning`)

### 4.2 Fallback Patterns

**YAML Backend Selection:**
- `storage.py:331-333` - `getattr(yaml, 'CSafeDumper', yaml.SafeDumper)` - Uses C-accelerated YAML if available, falls back to pure Python

**Environment Variable Defaults:**
- `jujucontext.py:97-103, 244-247` - `charm_dir` defaults to relative path if `JUJU_CHARM_DIR` missing
- `jujucontext.py:287-293` - `version` defaults to '0.0.0' for meter-status-changed events (Juju 2/3 compatibility)

---

## 5. CACHING PATTERNS

### 5.1 State Caching

**Relation Data Caching:**
- `model.py:1930-2008` - `RelationDataContent` implements `LazyMapping` with `_load()` method
- `_invalidate()` clears cache when relation data changes
- Caching justification: relation data is static during hook lifetime (safe to cache)

**Secret Content Caching:**
- `model.py:1473-1476` - `_content` cache attribute checked; `refresh=True` forces reload
- `peek_content()` at 1477-1492 bypasses cache for non-tracking scenarios

**Framework Type Registration:**
- `framework.py:register_type()` maintains type cache to avoid re-registering

### 5.2 Object Pooling

**Model Relation Instances:**
- `_private/harness.py:1120-1121, 1165-1166` - RelationDataContent lookup with `get()` to check if cached
- Cache invalidation on unit removal

---

## 6. RESOURCE CLEANUP PATTERNS

### 6.1 Context Manager Usage

**Database Connections:**
- `storage.py:62-75` - SQLiteStorage initializes with `isolation_level=None` (autocommit) and timeout settings
- `close()` method at line 112-114 for explicit cleanup
- `framework.py` wraps all storage operations in transaction context

**Framework Lifecycle:**
- `_main.py:488-496` - `_Manager.run()` calls `_emit()`, `_commit()`, `_close()` in try-finally with guaranteed `framework.close()`

**Pebble Temporary Files:**
- `pebble.py` uses context managers for file operations (implicit from `with open()`)

### 6.2 Explicit Cleanup Methods

**Storage Commit Pattern:**
- `storage.py:116-118` - `commit()` method on storage backends; framework calls this after successful event handling
- `_main.py:472-474` - `_Manager._commit()` explicitly calls `framework.commit()`

**Tracing/Observability Cleanup:**
- `_main.py:284-298` - Tracing setup with circular import break
- `_main.py:481-486` - `destroy()` method calls tracing shutdown in finally block

---

## 7. ARCHITECTURAL PATTERNS

### 7.1 Layered Architecture

**Five-Layer Design:**
1. **Charm Layer** (`charm.py`) - User-facing event handlers and metadata
2. **Framework Layer** (`framework.py`) - Event routing, object snapshotting, lifecycle
3. **Model Layer** (`model.py`) - Juju abstraction (Relations, Secrets, Storage, Units, Apps)
4. **Storage Layer** (`storage.py`) - Persistence backends (SQLite, Juju controller)
5. **Hook Commands Layer** (`hookcmds/`) - Direct Juju hook command wrappers

### 7.2 Backend Abstraction

**_ModelBackend Interface:**
- `model.py` - Abstract base with implementations for:
  - Real Juju execution (production)
  - Test harness mocking (`_private/harness.py`)
  - Scenario testing (`testing/mocking.py`)

**Storage Backend Abstraction:**
- `SQLiteStorage` - Local file-based persistence
- `JujuStorage` - Controller-side state storage
- Both implement identical interface: `save_snapshot()`, `load_snapshot()`, `drop_snapshot()`, `save_notice()`, `drop_notice()`, `notices()`

### 7.3 Event System Architecture

**Handle-Based Event Tracking:**
- `framework.py:77-160` - Hierarchical handle paths: `Kind[Key]/NestedKind[Key]`
- Handles used as keys for snapshot storage and event re-emission

**Event Deferral:**
- `framework.py:185-235` - `defer()` records handler in storage for re-execution
- Pattern: Framework re-emits deferred handlers before processing new event

---

## 8. TESTING INFRASTRUCTURE

### 8.1 Two Testing Approaches

**Harness Testing (Legacy):**
- `_private/harness.py` - Full charm lifecycle simulation with mock backend
- Provides `add_relation()`, `begin()`, `cleanup()`, `evaluate_status()`
- Mock relations, secrets, storage, pebble clients

**Scenario Testing (Modern):**
- `testing/scenario/` - State-transition testing with explicit input/output states
- `context.py` - Immutable State objects with deep copying of mutable fields
- `mocking.py` - Complete Juju/Pebble API mocking
- Tracing/observability built-in with OpenTelemetry

### 8.2 Mock Data Isolation

**State Deep Copying:**
- `testing/state.py:130-140` - Frozen dataclasses can't enforce immutability on nested structures, so mutable fields are deep-copied to prevent accidental sharing
- Testing framework maintains separate state for each event emission

---

## 9. PERFORMANCE CONSIDERATIONS

### 9.1 Lazy Loading

**Deferred Database Access:**
- Relation data, configuration, and secrets loaded on first access, not during model initialization
- Reduces Juju hook command invocations for unused data

### 9.2 Batch Operations

**Notice Collection:**
- `storage.py:165-170` - Uses `fetchmany()` for paginated iteration instead of `fetchall()`
- Prevents memory bloat with large event queues

### 9.3 Generator Patterns

**Iterator-Based Access:**
- `storage.py:161-170` - `list_snapshots()` returns generator
- `model.py:190-221` - `notices()` returns generator for notice iteration

---

## 10. KEY RISK AREAS & OBSERVATIONS

### High-Confidence Patterns
- ✓ Subprocess security is excellent (no shell execution, path lookup)
- ✓ File permissions correctly enforced on databases (0o600)
- ✓ Secret content handling via temp files prevents process env leakage
- ✓ All exception types documented and layered appropriately
- ✓ Version compatibility checking comprehensive and explicit

### Areas Requiring Care
- **Pickle Deserialization**: Uses `pickle.loads()` on snapshot data; safe because source is SQLite under full charm control, but marked with `# noqa: S301` indicating known security consideration
- **YAML Loading**: Custom loaders restrict to basic types, but still marked `# noqa: S506` for unsafe YAML
- **Deep Copy Semantics**: Some operations deep copy while others shallow copy; consistency could be improved
- **Cache Invalidation**: Lazy mapping invalidation is manual (`_invalidate()` calls); potential for stale data if invalidation missed

### Notable Design Decisions
1. **No Rollback on Storage**: Comment at `storage.py:120-124` explains deliberate choice to not implement rollback
2. **Frozen Dataclasses with Mutable Fields**: Compromise to allow testing while providing some immutability guarantees
3. **Tracing Context Manual Entry/Exit**: Rather than using `with` statement (line 297-298), manually calls `__enter__`/`__exit__` to ensure cleanup on all paths
4. **Defensive Copying**: Extensive use of `.copy()` and `copy.deepcopy()` as standard practice rather than exception

---

## Summary Table: Pattern Distribution

| Concern Area | Primary Location | Pattern Type | Coverage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Copying** | model.py, pebble.py, testing/state.py | Defensive/Deep/Shallow | Comprehensive |
| **Error Handling** | _main.py, storage.py, hookcmds/ | Layered exceptions, validation | Comprehensive |
| **Security** | storage.py, hookcmds/ | Permissions, secret files, no shell | High |
| **Compatibility** | jujuversion.py, _main.py | Version checks, fallbacks | Good coverage for supported versions |
| **Caching** | model.py, framework.py | Lazy loading, manual invalidation | Used selectively |
| **Cleanup** | _main.py, storage.py, hookcmds/ | Context managers, explicit close | Comprehensive |
| **Architecture** | framework.py, model.py | Layered, abstract backends | Well-structured |
| **Testing** | testing/, _private/harness.py | Two approaches, state isolation | Excellent |</result>

fix: only show executable in ExecError.__str__, not full command line (#2336)
2026-02-23 08:48:38 +1300
This is to avoid credentials or sensitive data passed in command line
args showing up in exception messages and logs. Sensitive data
*shouldn't* be passed in command line args, but it sometimes is, so
better to be cautious here. We do something similar with tracing: only
send the executable name for pebble exec.

fix: prevent `KeyError` on `auth-type` when creating `CloudCredential` object (#2268)
2026-01-14 21:04:58 +0100
Fixes #2267

When the hook command `credential-get` is executed as part of ops'
`model.get_cloud_spec()`, it currently fails with `KeyError` (see issue
linked above).

This PR drops the use of `dataclasses.asdict` to get a Juju-style
dictionary (to then convert into ops.model classes), since that doesn't
undo the `-` to `_` that happened when making the data classes. Instead,
new private methods to convert from a hookcmds class are added and used.

0422491787a573de4b2ac527c5b85a32521f2bef
fix: detect categories with an explanation mark indicating breaking changes (#2132)
2025-10-22 15:23:17 +1300
Currently, the release script fails to recognise lines with the `!`
breaking change indicator in the category, meaning they are not included
in the change log or release notes (and yet, are probably the most
important to include).

The PR:
* Changes the regular expression to use named groups for better
readability.
* Changes the regular expression to capture the `!` if present.
* Puts any breaking changes into a separate category (keeping the main
category in the description).
* In the changelog, puts the changes into a "Breaking Changes" group.
* In the release notes, adds a top-level section that calls out that
there are breaking changes, and lists them, and notifies the user about
this, because it's likely that some hand-crafted text will be required
to cover these.

Also, to handle the upcoming release, adds support for alpha, beta, and
release candidate numbers in the version (in a fairly minimal way - the
tag, the title, and generally not crashing).

Fixes #2111

6aaacfdf7322ccc258c6fcdf19d74a3b3e25ade4
fix: raise ActionFailed when using Context as a context manager (#2121)
2025-10-21 10:40:56 +1300
This PR ensures that `ActionFailed` is raised both when using
`Context.run()` directly, and when it is used as a context manager, by
moving the relevant code to a method common to both techniques.

To reproduce the issue, start with this setup:

```python
>>> from ops import testing
>>> import ops
>>> class C(ops.CharmBase):
...     def __init__(self, framework):
...         super().__init__(framework)
...         framework.observe(self.on['act'].action, self._on_act)
...     def _on_act(self, event):
...         event.fail("Failed")
...         
```

With a regular run, when an action fails, `ActionFailed` is raised. This
is the expected behaviour:

```python
>>> ctx = testing.Context(C, meta={'name': 'foo'}, actions={'act': {}})
>>> ctx.run(ctx.on.action('act'), testing.State())
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<python-input-5>", line 1, in <module>
    ctx.run(ctx.on.action('act'), testing.State())
    ~~~~~~~^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/home/tameyer/w/operator/testing/src/scenario/context.py", line 830, in run
    raise ActionFailed(
    ...<2 lines>...
    )
ops._private.harness.ActionFailed: Failed
```

However, when using `Context` as a context manager, it is not:

```python
>>> with ctx(ctx.on.action('act'), testing.State()) as mgr:
...     mgr.run()
...     
State(config={}, relations=frozenset(), networks=frozenset(), containers=frozenset(), storages=frozenset(), opened_ports=frozenset(), leader=False, model=Model(name='dETzC56WsRPvSb3pDPY8', uuid='912539dd-57d4-453d-9e90-166d04fef696', type='kubernetes', cloud_spec=None), secrets=frozenset(), resources=frozenset(), planned_units=1, deferred=[], stored_states=frozenset({StoredState(name='_stored', owner_path=None, content={'event_count': 4}, _data_type_name='StoredStateData')}), app_status=UnknownStatus(), unit_status=UnknownStatus(), workload_version='')
```

The issue here is that `Manager.run()` and `Context.run()` use
`Context._run()` to call `ops.run()` (too many "run"s!), but the code
that handles raising `ActionFailed` (and setting the logs and action
messages) is only in `Context.run()`.

---------

Co-authored-by: James Garner <james.garner@canonical.com>

 testing/tests/test_e2e/test_actions.py | 29 +++++++++++++++++++++++++++++
 2 files changed, 44 insertions(+), 14 deletions(-)

5a63e01e2b2eec9e23f226f08f9c47c6ddeb3e0a
fix: only add the remote unit for departed and broken relation events, fix ordering (#1918)
2025-07-28 12:13:43 +1200
We inject the `JUJU_REMOTE_UNIT` unit into the relation's unit so that
the databag is available during relation-departed events. When this was
added in #1364 the thinking was that it was cleaner to just always add
that unit rather than check for the specific events. However, that was
not correct: this means that the unit may be added before the data is
available (in relation joined, for example).

This PR changes the injection so that it only happens in relation
departed and relation broken events.

In addition, we inserted the unit into the relation *after* setting up
the relation's `.data`, which meant that the data would not be
accessible for the inserted unit. The PR adjusts the ordering so that
the inserting is done first.

Fixes #1917

31091898e169032f7165afe77157be982da521ea
fix: in meter-status-changed JUJU_VERSION is not set (#1840)
2025-06-24 13:13:28 +1200
Despite the
[documentation](https://documentation.ubuntu.com/juju/3.6/reference/hook/)
saying that `JUJU_VERSION` is set in the environment for all hooks, it
is not for the `meter-status-changed` or `collect-metrics` events.

Restore the older behaviour (prior to #1313) where if `JUJU_VERSION` is
not available we set a version of `0.0.0`. We can drop this once ops
requires Juju 4 and above.

In addition, in the restricted context the `is-leader` hook tool is not
available, which means that collect-status fails (before emitting any
events, because it can't figure out which events to emit). We could just
do the unit status in this case, but it seems simpler to just skip
collect status in this case (which is only for two events no-one should
be using any more).

Finally, the PR adds `meter-status-changed` to the list of restricted
context events, which means that we won't run any deferred events during
those hooks (as with `collect-metrics`), and will error out cleanly if
`use_juju_for_storage` is used. Not emitting deferred events is a
breaking change, but definitely should be done - it's extremely likely
that the handler would crash if unexpectedly running in a restricted
context. Similarly, for the `use_juju_for_storage` case, the charm would
have less gracefully crashed anyway, when ops tried to use storage
itself.

Fixes #1836

668701b4396098b3e7a93288cd272c11dd2a8af6
fix: turn on databag access validation in __init__ (#1737)
2025-05-28 17:02:26 +1200
This PR sets the event context during charm initialisation. The event
name is set to a dummy event name, `'__init__'`. Setting the event name
to a non-falsey value enables relation data access validation during the
charm's `__init__` method.

This means that at runtime, if we don't have permission to access a
databag in `__init__`, we will pre-emptively raise a
`RelationDataAccessError` (a subclass of `ModelError`) like we do in
observers, instead of just raising a plain `ModelError` after the Juju
hook tools run. In Scenario and Harness we will raise a
`RelationDataAccessError` instead of silently passing.

Resolves: #1378

3dda5b5f64bdea21d43a42431edcca62669c3173
fix: assorted fixes for Pebble layer merging in Harness and Scenario (#1627)
2025-03-14 16:31:53 +1300
* When merging `pebble.Check`s, an unset `startup` should not override a
set `startup`. (Once set you can't go back to unset - you need to
explicitly use enabled).
* When rendering services, checks, and log targets from a series of
layers, Harness and Scenario were combining after sorting the items by
name. Although Pebble uses filename sorting when reading initial layers
from a directory, future layers are combined in the order they are added
via the API. For Harness and Scenario, we need to use the order in which
they were added - if the caller wants to simulate loading from the
filesystem, they need to do that by calling `add_layer` in that order.
The plan itself still sorts the services, checks, and log targets.
* When rendering services, checks, and log targets, if there are
multiple services/checks/log-targets with the same name, and `override`
is set to `merge`, the service/check/log-target needs to merge rather
than replace. This was already done when adding layers with the same
name (in `add_layer`) but not when there were differently named layers.
* The Scenario `Container.plan` property was ignoring any defined checks
and log targets. Those are now included.
* The Scenario `Container` service, check, and log target rendering also
needed the (no) sorting and merging fixes from above.
* When adding a layer in Harness or Scenario, update any existing
`CheckInfo`s to match changes to the plan - in real Pebble the startup,
level, and threshold fields are always pulled from the plan but we bake
them in when testing, so need to make sure they continually track what's
in the combined plan.

Also adds a `__repr__` for `pebble.Plan` - this helped while debugging
issues, and it seems reasonable to keep it.

---------

Co-authored-by: Ben Hoyt <benhoyt@gmail.com>

d14c30331bc4fdde0d5a880475ace04fe6b5d455
fix: maintain transaction integrity on first run (#1558)
2025-02-03 17:47:22 +0900
Fixing the db transaction when the db is being created in this dispatch
call.

We commit the whole unit state on_commit()

If the DDL is committed early, then the rest of the operations are
performed in the autocommit mode, potentially breaking the transactional
guarantees the rest of the code assumes.

Meanwhile it is safe (and normal) to modify DDL within a transaction in
sqlite.

Additionally, I feel that the transaction code was added to make doubly
sure that two hooks cannot be run concurrently... Doesn't Juju guarantee
that for us anyway?

79706f401d93a4e85b729de52a81367f6d3f6451
fix: adjust Harness secret behaviour to align with Juju (#1248)
2024-06-25 15:45:08 +1200
Adjust Harness so that the errors match what Juju does in practice (I
tested each case - the results are in a table in a comment in #1229).
* When Juju responds with a message including "not found", we raise
`SecretNotFoundError`
* When Juju responds with any other message (such as "permission
denied"), we raise `ModelError`
* When Juju succeeds and only fails at the completion of the hook, we
raise `RuntimeError` - in production, the charm cannot catch this, but
we do want to surface this error, so `RuntimeError` seems most
approprate

In addition:
* Update the documentation to clarify when secret content is cached
(this was a request from the discussion with the data platform team in
Madrid)
* Correct the documentation regarding when `ModelError` and when
`SecretNotFoundError` will be raised with the secret methods
* Don't clear the `Secret` object local cache of the secret content on
`set_contents`. The contents won't change until `refresh` is used, so
there's no point forcing the next call to get the content from Juju.

Juju may change the responses so that there is increased consistency
(probably via [this bug](https://bugs.launchpad.net/juju/+bug/2067336))
but it seems best if we fix the behaviour to match now, and then if Juju
changes in the future, we can update ops then (also deciding at that
time how to handle the issue that different Juju versions will have
different behaviour).

Fixes #1229.

commit 85e677ef38856f54978a70a6341b2b544aa2547e
Author: Copilot <198982749+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>
Date:   Fri Jan 23 16:50:44 2026 +1300

    fix: replace git reference injection with wheel artifacts in charm test workflows (#2252)
    
    For our compatibility tests, instead of patching the dependencies to
    require the branch's version of ops[...] via a Git link, build local
    wheels from the branch and patch the requirements to use that. This
    avoids races where the appropriate revision of the branch is not yet
    available from the CI runner.
    
    Changes:
    * [x] Create a reusable workflow to build ops wheels (ops, ops-scenario,
    ops-tracing)
    * [x] Update observability-charm-tests.yaml to use wheel artifacts
    instead of git references
    * [x] Update published-charms-tests.yaml to use wheel artifacts instead
    of git references
    * [x] Update db-charm-tests.yaml to use wheel artifacts instead of git
    references
    * [x] Update hello-charm-tests.yaml to use wheel artifacts instead of
    git references
    
    - Fixes canonical/operator#1843
